frameworks.name: LLM-as-collaborator analogy
frameworks.one_line: Schulz et al. frame working with LLMs as comparable to working with human collaborators

frameworks.name: critical-limitation perspective
frameworks.one_line: Bender et al. argue LLMs are misused and overhyped, favoring specialized interpretable tools

frameworks.name: responsible-use / attribution ethics
frameworks.one_line: Marelli et al. emphasize transparent attribution and responsible scientific use of LLMs

frameworks.name: human-led scientific roadmap
frameworks.one_line: Botvinick and Gershman argue humans must retain responsibility for scientific direction

core_question: How should the advancement of large language models affect the practice of science?
key_assumptions: LLMs are increasingly being incorporated into scientific workflows
the implications of LLM integration are not yet fully understood
diverse expert perspectives can illuminate the tradeoffs
manipulations: juxtaposition of four contrasting expert positions
comparison of collaborator-analogy vs limitation-focused views
key_findings: experts disagree on whether LLM use resembles human collaboration
some argue LLMs are overhyped and limitations favor specialized tools
transparent attribution is highlighted as essential
humans should remain in charge of the scientific roadmap
interpretations: the role of LLMs in science is contested and not settled
responsible use requires attribution, interpretability, and human oversight
